Alright, welcome back. So we are gonna kick things off with a really exciting, really sexy, really incredible topic called ethics. Yes. No, for real. I mean, I get kind of lit up with this, right? Because when you are clear on your ethical code, right, what you will or won't stand for, all of a sudden you have this greater sense of self permission, right, to show up on these calls, you're no longer kind of worried about, can I do this? Will I do this? Won't I do that? And ultimately, right, if you are the empathetic type who doesn't want to put your prospect in a place of feeling shitty, feeling scared, feeling unsafe, all these things, right, knowing your ethical stance and what that looks like in a very practical sense actually liberates you to feel more free, feel more liberated yourself on these calls and really kind of stand by yourself and stand by your program in a more compelling way, in a more leadership based way as you lead them through the aspects of an empowered decision. So that's what ethics can do. I certainly won't tell you what yours are or aren't or should or shouldn't be. I'm not here to preach all this is for you to ultimately decide based on where you're at, based on what feels good to you. So I'll simply offer mine as a reference point. But the litmus, sorry, the litmus test is in living by a set of ethics that essentially make you and your prospect feel amazing on every call. So that's typically how you will know and feel whether or not your ethical code right now is really kind of up to par and where it needs to be, right, these calls should feel amazing, they should feel liberating, they should feel leadership based, regardless of the final outcome, right, you should feel incredible getting off of it. And that's really a way to kind of know whether or not your ethics are in service of that. So why do we need an ethical code keyword need we actually do need one right. Well, first we got to acknowledge three things we have to acknowledge that you're likely at an intellectual advantage, you know, having studied sales marketing and persuasion these are skills that you have that put you at a conversational or even a rhetoric or persuasive advantage, right, and that's okay to acknowledge it's also okay to acknowledge that you're at an emotional advantage, you are asking them to open up and get vulnerable about their situation while you provide a sturdy container for it right so they are at kind of a more emotionally vulnerable open place than you are that kind of does create a bit of a disbalance there, and then acknowledge that you're at an expertise advantage you are a natural, you are in a natural position of authority and expertise around this topic right there will be greater trust in what you proclaim what you say the consequences will or won't be right because you have that advantage as well. So between these three conditions or advantages, it's also okay to acknowledge that this creates an inherent imbalance of power with the potential to abuse it even subtly, unless we've created our own set of rules that there's nothing inherently wrong with this imbalance of power, but you know, to miss quote or to poorly quote or to over quote, Uncle Ben, you know, great power, great responsibility. I couldn't even saying that. But to make this all very practical, three things we won't be doing, and this is what's going to really cover the 8020, the 9010, the 955 of where potential abuses of power can occur. They typically fall under these three things right sales driven gaslighting, fear mongering and exaggeration of consequences and forced compliance, aka the high pressure close. So these are the three that we are going to unpack a little bit more right now and show you how they actually show up what they look like and why you may not want to do them. Your choice, of course. Cool. So sales driven gaslighting. This is essentially mirroring back a listing shame and forcing consistency by drawing a false equivalency between an earlier low barrier micro commitment and the enrollment opportunity. So you may want to reread that a few times, but it's all in there. So this is when you mirror back a listed shame, even on a subtle level and force consistency by drawing false equivalency between what they said earlier in a low barrier, lighter, more casual environment and the enrollment opportunity, which is a totally different context. So how this plays out early in the call on a scale of one to 10, how important is it to you that you enrich your relationship with your partner and find greater connection, love and intimacy. Now this is a no brainer, right prospects who are on that call will say 10, right. And then they'll be probed around why this matters and essentially be led to imagine consequences of not resolving it. And then later in the call, when the prospect hits appropriate resistance around you, around your program, around maybe a $10,000 relationship coaching program, he or she will be reminded, right, right, you were a 10 before. Maybe I'm misunderstanding. Wasn't this important to you? Now let that sit. This is a very common tactic. And given this definition of sales driven gaslighting, can you see how we are drawing false equivalency between that outcome being important and them being essentially gaslit into compliance on the program? Right. So this line of false equivalency and gaslighting creates a disempowering state that's shaming, right. You're kind of being shamed around. I thought this was important to you patronizing same thing and condescending. It has the person questioning their own recollection of the conversation, which creates a sense of being disoriented. And it is disorienting because the false equivalent, the false equivalency doesn't compute it doesn't register it literally doesn't make sense. And if they believe you as the expert and the authority, all of a sudden they've bypassed their own trust, their own self trust. So when you're disoriented and untrusting of yourself, you're disempowered. That is what it means in a deeper sense. And this is essentially where compliance begins. Next, fear mongering. So this is creating imagined or hyper exaggerated consequences of inaction or leaving the problem unresolved. So another very typical common tactic that is just kind of taken as the standard of what we do on sales calls. And yes, let's acknowledge there are very real consequences to inaction. And yes, in many cases, they can actually be quite devastating if left unresolved. However, creating exactness and certainty into manifestation of those consequences, if they don't join your program, it's once again creating a false equivalency. So once again, example, if you don't take action now with my program in three months from now, I see you being in a deeper hole with your energy levels and likely losing your business due to your inability to keep up with its demands. Is that really a choice that you're actually willing to make? Now, is this actually true? Or is this drawing a false equivalency? And what would be more true? If you don't take control of your chronic fatigue symptoms, it's quite possible. It's perhaps likely that your energy levels will further deplete and be harder to regain, preventing you from having the necessary energy and focused attention to scale your business while raising your family. So one of them is creating more of a destiny and linking it to the false equivalency with taking your program. So taking your program will result in that versus if you don't take control of your chronic fatigue symptoms. That may or may not be through this program. That may or may not be through you. And it's far more accurate and true to this situation. So how to ethically avoid fear-mongering? Three very simple steps on that. One, be real and honest about what might happen. Ask them to explore what they believe things may devolve or decay into rather than you essentially feeding them this very fear-based, exaggerated destiny of what absolutely will happen. Get them to explore what may happen. Someone is going to adopt their own truth and realization and insights a lot faster, a lot easier than they're going to take yours on. Especially if they get the sense that what you're sharing may not be absolutely true. Explore if they have any other thoughts or options at this time of how to approach it rather than creating the false equivalency where your program is the only thing in the world, the only option that they can solve that problem with and if they don't take your program, all this shitty stuff is going to happen. Awesome. If you don't join my program, if you don't take this opportunity for not working together, do you currently have any other ways to address this problem or achieve this outcome? That essentially makes it very clear that it's not an ultimatum between your program, take my program, or else all this shitty stuff is going to happen. And ultimately we want to avoid creating that false destiny that equates not buying today with the certainty of consequences, especially when they do have other opportunities to resolve it and they're actively considering those as well. So this is really, once again, the 90-10 of how you're going to avoid fear mongering while still ethically exploring potential consequences of what may happen and what might even be likely to happen if they don't move forward and if they don't have any other active options in terms of solving the problem. So why are we doing this? We don't want decisions based in fear. I'm going to scrape that out for the people in the back. We don't want decisions based in fear and why? Because fear is a decision handicap. It creates low value decisions and low value decisions ultimately hurt us one way or another. This is explored. This is quoted from an incredible book, A World Without Fear by John Jones. It's one of these kind of like under the radar self-published things that you'll never actually see in a bookstore or on an Amazon list. So I do propose that you check it out both for your ability to understand the mechanisms of fear a little bit better and maybe even release some of your own, which would be cool. So we also want to avoid the high pressure, close at all costs, the forced compliance, right? And this is where they're preying on fear, false scarcity, false urgency, disorientation, disempowerment to make one bypass their intuition, judgment and decision making capacities and ultimately give in to forced compliance. This is a word we're going to use a lot because I do believe that up to like 50% of sales that are actually closed aren't empowered. They're just versions of forced compliance. So what most consider to be a close, I'd say is really forced compliance. The litmus test for this is if your prospect is less energized, less empowered, less excited, less expansive, less expressive of their own energy, then how they began the call. You didn't empower a decision. You actually forced compliance. So high pressure is what drives this need for forced compliance. And it's usually actually the result of insecurity and fear on the part of the salesperson that if the person escapes and you kind of have to ask, what are they escaping from? The sale will be lost. So this is really insecurity based on the part of the salesperson that they need to get the decision. They need to get the yes on this call right now. Because once that person escapes, once the prey escapes, the Conquest is over and they're going to run as far as they freaking can away from you. They're going to ghost your messages with good reason. Cool. So that's food for thought. What are they escaping from in this context? And if you're afraid of someone not responding to you, not answering your follow ups, ghosting on you once you get off the line, that's a good opportunity for a self check, right? What are they escaping from? And how can you make yourself somebody who someone else doesn't feel they need to run the fuck away from? Cool. Food for thought. So we want an empowered not right now, more than a disempowered yes. And the former is actually more profitable. And when you have the right follow up systems and processes in place, like the Tyler dirt and follow up, which we'll talk about later, you can safely play it at whatever pace your prospect needs and feels best in for their journey. So there's a paradox here. The paradox is that when you give this safety and looseness with directional leadership, decisions are actually made faster. Why? Right? So fear is a decision making handicap, right? But if they're in a relaxed and an expansive state and can embrace wider perspective and possibility, whereas pressure creates defense, armoring, contraction, which limits their ability to actually perceive possibility, right? So we want to create this relaxed and expansive state. We do that with appropriate looseness, right? Allowing people to make decisions on their own terms with the right leadership and follow up mechanisms in place. So that's what we're going for. There is a dark side to forced compliance. We alluded to this actually being less profitable. But when someone joined from a place of fear, pressure, disempowerment and stress, they'll suppress it, right? So we want to acknowledge that they felt all these things, but it will be bundled active energy that you as their coach are implicated in. Implicated in. So it won't disappear all of a sudden on the first coaching call or upon getting access login to your program or your first appointment, whatever that looks like. They'll actually be in your program with a learning disability and certainly an implementation disability because their self-confidence, their self-trust, their personal power has essentially been traumatized. And diminished by you and being in your presence. Can you see how this works now? Cool. So the practical cost of that is they'll be in a state of blame, shock and victimhood right from the start. Why? Because you've coached them to be in relationship with you in that state. And now you're demanding that all of a sudden they take empowered action after you've literally siphoned it from their being and from their bodies on the sales call. So this is where you pay for it. And I'm not talking about you, you. I'm talking about this imaginary sales person who is forcing compliance, but this is where they pay for it in clients who don't get results. And maybe if they're smart, actually refuse to pay or literally can't, you know, and who tax your fulfillment team and you along the process. So this is the practical cost of forced compliance that a lot of businesses who are still operating under this sales paradigm end up paying for in the long term. So in short, the short term gain from forced compliance is definitely offset in fulfillment. Of course, that's saying nothing to the actual ethical cost of running your business that way and how that leads out right to your reputation and your own integrity. So yes, you can argue and you can make a very compelling argument that everyone is responsible for their own decisions. And on a deep soul level, I fully agree. Right. But if we can also agree that not everyone is in their full sovereign power and that some folks you talk to are traumatized, exhausted, vulnerable and sometimes even made more so by talking to you. Anything less than acknowledging and self checking around your ethics is a gateway to being predatory in this context. So this is why this is a necessary conversation. And this is where I invite you to check in right knowing that these three tactics we broke down of gaslighting and fear mongering and forced compliance are no longer available to you in this program. And if you run the frameworks we're going to teach. Do you yourself right as someone who's going to be leading these calls feel more handicapped and straight jacketed in your ability to sell. Or do you feel more expansive, relieved and excited for your next call, knowing that these things are off the table, right and you get to actually probably reassume a more natural state of being a way that you actually don't need to convince yourself isn't predatory. When your wiser self, you know, actually recognizes that it is so this is your opportunity to check in. How does it make you feel knowing that we're no longer going to be doing those things. If it feels great. It feels amazing. I have good news for you. We are going to usher in a new way of doing things and new way of showing up a new way of actually unpacking what is real and true in your prospect and the leading them towards empowered decisions. On their timeline from a place of relaxness, a place of empowering them with everything they need mentally, emotionally to make those decisions. And we're going to feel freaking awesome doing it. So that's what we got. Thanks for being here. Take a breather and I'll meet you in the next lesson. Back for now.
